title: Listening - Part 4: Returning to Work After a Break
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people talking about their experiences of re-entering the workforce after an extended absence

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d spent six years at home raising my kids, and [21]I chose to return to work mainly because I missed the intellectual stimulation and adult conversation of an office[/21]. I felt my brain was turning to mush! It was a tough transition. [26]The biggest hurdle was the steep learning curve with all the new software that had been introduced while I was away[/26]. I felt like a dinosaur for the first few months. I had to spend my evenings watching tutorials just to keep up with my younger colleagues. It was humbling, but eventually, I got the hang of it and regained my professional confidence. || Speaker 2: I’d taken a three-year break to care for an elderly parent, and [22]I went back to work because I found that our family finances were becoming increasingly strained[/22]. We needed the extra income. I was lucky to find a part-time role. However, [27]I’ve found it incredibly difficult to manage the lingering feelings of guilt about leaving my parent with a professional carer[/27]. Even though she’s well-looked after, I constantly check my phone for messages. It makes it hard to focus 100% on my tasks, and I often feel like I’m failing at both roles. || Speaker 3: After a serious illness, I was away from my career for eighteen months. [23]I decided to return to work because I felt that having a routine would help my overall recovery and mental health[/23]. I needed a reason to get out of bed. [28]What I didn't expect was the overwhelming kindness and support shown by my managers[/28]. They didn't pressure me at all and allowed me to gradually increase my hours. It made me realize how much I valued my workplace community. I’m much more loyal to the company now than I ever was before my illness. || Speaker 4: I’d been traveling the world for two years, and [24]I only went back to work because I’d completely run out of savings and had no other choice[/24]. I was quite resentful at first. [29]The most challenging aspect was the sudden lack of freedom and the feeling of being trapped in a cubicle all day[/29]. I missed the spontaneity of my travels. I’ve had to make a real effort to find small ways to enjoy my weekends just to stay sane. I’m already planning my next big trip, using this job as a means to an end. || Speaker 5: I’d tried to start my own business during my break, but it didn't work out. [25]I returned to a corporate role because I realized I preferred the security of a steady paycheck over the stress of entrepreneurship[/25]. I wanted stability. [30]The most rewarding part has been the discovery that my time away actually made me a more efficient and focused worker[/30]. I no longer waste time on office politics or unnecessary meetings. I just want to get my work done and go home. My boss has actually commented on how much my productivity has improved compared to before I left. It was a valuable lesson.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) each speaker's main reason for returning to work.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 missed intellectual stimulation and adult conversation.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 returned because family finances were becoming strained.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 felt a routine would help his recovery and mental health.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 returned because she had run out of savings and had no choice.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted the security and stability of a steady paycheck.

options.C: to regain a sense of job security
options.G: to escape a boring home life
options.H: to fund a future personal project

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions about their re-entry into the workplace.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions the struggle with new software and technology.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 struggles with guilt about leaving her parent with a carer.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 was surprised by the kindness and support of his managers.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 found the sudden lack of freedom and being in a cubicle challenging.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 discovered he was more efficient and focused after his time away.

options.A: the difficulty of losing their independence
options.B: the emotional burden of their home life
options.C: a conflict with a younger supervisor
options.D: a sense of being overqualified for the role
options.E: the pleasure of learning new skills
options.F: a surprising increase in their work rate
options.H: the appreciative attitude of their employers
